retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Hysterectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Urinary bladder and ureteral injuries are the most common preventable complications that can occur during the hysterectomy. The technique for performing a hysterectomy is as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Hysterectomy -- Complications -- Genitourinary and Gastrointestinal Tract Injuries
retrieved_docs.content: Injury to the genitourinary tract during pelvic surgery, while rare, can lead to a high risk of patient morbidity. Studies have indicated radical hysterectomy as the most common type of pelvic surgery associated with urologic complications. [29] The bladder is injured more frequently than the ureters. A review of urinary tract injuries during benign gynecologic surgery found lower rates of bladder injury after abdominal hysterectomy than after laparoscopic and vaginal approaches, consistent with other studies within the literature. [30] Injury to the bladder occurs most commonly during dissection within the vesicovaginal plane, whereas injury to the ureter is most common to occur during dissection along the pelvic sidewall, particularly when encountering the infundibulopelvic ligaments where the ovarian vessels are ligated, but also during ligation of the uterine vessels and at the bladder base. [24] While injuries to the bladder and ureter may be noted during surgery, injury to the serosal layer of the bladder may go unnoticed during surgery if the defect in the bladder wall is not full-thickness, and delayed presentation of vesicovaginal fistula can occur. [31] GI tract injuries during an abdominal hysterectomy can occur via thermal injury, direct mechanical damage, and indirectly through interruption of vascular supply.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteral Injury -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The overall incidence of iatrogenic ureteral injuries caused during operative procedures varies from 0.5 to 10%. Analysis of 13 published studies concluded the following procedures contribute to iatrogenic ureteral injuries: hysterectomy (54%), colorectal surgery (14%), pelvic procedures such as ovarian tumor removal (8%), transabdominal urethropexy (8%), and abdominal vascular surgery (6%). Another retrospective review identified 55 patients with iatrogenic ureteral injuries during a 16 year period and noted the majority of ureteral injuries occurred during gynecologic procedures (55%). The remainder of procedures were urologic (25%), colorectal (15%), and vascular (5%).

retrieved_docs.title: Oophorectomy -- Complications -- Genitourinary Tract
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary tract injuries occur in 1% to 2% of gynecologic surgeries, with higher rates in patients with predisposing risk factors. [19] Unfortunately, most ureteral injuries go unrecognized intraoperatively, making heightened awareness during the postoperative period essential.

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Hysterectomy -- Complications -- A. Intraoperative Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Bleeding - The most common sites of bleeding during vaginal hysterectomy are uterine vessels, Utero-ovarian ligament, and vaginal cuff. [4] Ureteral injury- The incidence of ureteral injury is about 0.5 percent. [6] Bladder injury- The prevalence of bladder injury during vaginal hysterectomy is up to 1.2 percent. It increases with risk factors like prior pelvic surgeries and concomitant bladder surgery. [7] Bowel injury- The risk is approximately 0.4 percent. Nerve injuries- Most commonly, the femoral nerve, peroneal, and tibial nerves are affected by the retractors or by malposition of the legs on the stirrups. Conversion to laparotomy- Instances like unexpected large pelvic masses, adhesions, and hemorrhage unable to identify and control can increase the chances of conversion to abdominal hysterectomy. Adverse reactions to anesthetics

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The most common causes of ureteral injury are iatrogenic. [29] The overall incidence of iatrogenic ureteral injury varies between 0.5% and 10%. The type of procedure that most frequently causes inadvertent ureteral injury is a hysterectomy (54%) due to the proximity of the uterine artery to the distal ureter. [30] [31] Each ureter is typically only about 2 cm from the cervix, but the distance can be shorter than 0.5 cm in 12% of women. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Hysterectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Cystoscopy : Postoperative visualization of proper flow within the ureters is recommended during instances where the ureter is not well visualized during the procedure or when there is suspicion for injury. Injury detection rates may be higher with the use of cystoscopy, and some studies urge its routine use after abdominal hysterectomy, given the increased morbidity and costs associated with unrecognized or delayed diagnosis of urinary tract injury, despite the increased procedural time. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Lower Genitourinary Trauma -- Treatment / Management -- Female Organs
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned above, injury to a non-gravid uterus is exceedingly rare. Most occur due to penetrating trauma, and thus generally necessitate laparotomy for other injuries. Blunt trauma can cause uterine injury. The uterus should be repaired with a two-layer closure using absorbable suture. Blast injury to the uterus may require a hysterectomy. Hysterectomy may also be required if the uterine artery is damaged. Uterine artery damage has also been treated with oxytocin. Fallopian tube or ovarian lacerations are repaired primarily with absorbable suture. If the damage is too extensive, then salpingo-oophorectomy should be performed. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis Ureter -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopic hysterectomies cause more iatrogenic ureteral injuries than open surgeries. Placement of bilateral ureteral catheters or stents reduces this risk but lengthens the overall procedure and produces more genitourinary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteral Injury -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A urologist usually manages ureteral injuries, but the cause of ureteral injury can occur during a variety of surgical procedures. An iatrogenic ureteral injury most commonly occurs during gynecologic surgery (52% to 82%). Hysterectomy accounts for the majority of cases resulting in ureteral injury. Injury occurs in the distal ureter in the region of the infundibulopelvic ligament, where the ureters cross inferior to the uterine artery. If an injury occurs during laparoscopic gynecologic surgery, it is usually caused by cauterization or clipping.

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Hysterectomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Bladder decompression: A Foley catheter is inserted and remains postoperatively until the patient is ambulatory. Other preparations to consider in special circumstances include preserved blood preparation in cases where a large amount of bleeding is predicted and urinary stent placement if there is a high risk for ureteral injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteral Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic ureteral stents/catheters can be placed prior to surgery to assist in intraoperative identification of ureters. This is often performed in surgeries that occur in close proximity to the ureter, such as gynecologic, rectosigmoid, and aortoiliac surgeries and particularly important if the operative field is scarred from previous dissection. It is debated whether stents actually prevent ureteral injury. In one review of 5729 colectomies, there was no difference seen for those who did versus did not undergo prophylactic ureteral stent placement. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Uterine Arteries -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: During a hysterectomy, it is important to isolate the descending ureter from the uterine artery to avoid injury to the ureter. A surgical injury to the lower ureter happens during a difficult surgery, such as in cases of a tubo-ovarian abscess, pelvic mass or various malignant conditions of pelvic organs. Many times, injuries of the ureter are missed during surgery. Postoperative cystoscopy and visualization of the free flow of a dye from both ureters may assure intact ureters. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Ureteral Injury -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of injury to the ureter may be lower for laparoscopic versus open surgical approaches. A review looking at over 90,000 colectomies showed the incidence of ureteral injury in laparoscopic approach to be 0.54% compared with 0.66% with open surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Cancer (Archived) -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical oncologists play a very important role in the management of patients with endometrial carcinoma. Total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the treatment of choice in patients with stage I disease. The most common complication associated with total abdominal hysterectomy is an excessive loss of blood. Other complications include infection, side effects of anesthesia, bladder rupture, and ureter damage. Sexual function remains unaffected after treatment. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality is recognized as an allowable qualifier for a range of events and surgical interventions, including the accident (حادث) and several procedures involving the female reproductive system. The procedures hysterectomy (إزالة الرحم), ovariectomy (استئصال المبيض), uterine dilation and curettage (توسيع الرحم وكشطه), and other excision of cervix and uterus are all classified under the broader category of procedures for female reproductive organs (عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية). This hierarchical relationship indicates that each of these specific surgeries is a subtype of the general female reproductive organ procedure, and mortality can be used as a metric to assess outcomes for each. Recognizing mortality as an allowed qualifier for these interventions supports systematic reporting and risk assessment in clinical practice.

This co‑occurrence reflects a clinical approach where both organs are addressed in a single operative session to treat disease, reduce patient morbidity, and streamline postoperative recovery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) and partial removal of the uterus (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا) are surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur in clinical practice. Similarly, bilateral removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) also commonly co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا). The repeated association of these gynecologic surgeries indicates that, when extensive pelvic pathology or prophylactic indications are present, clinicians often combine ovarian or salpingo‑oophorectomy with a partial hysterectomy to address multiple reproductive organs in a single operative session, thereby streamlining patient care and reducing the need for separate procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2294
retrieved_docs.content: OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of repair of current obstetric laceration, indicating that it encompasses procedures specifically aimed at correcting lacerations that occur during obstetric events. The concept of repair of current obstetric laceration is itself a type of عملية في الرحم (uterine surgery), situating these obstetric repair techniques within the broader context of surgical interventions performed on the uterus. This hierarchical relationship highlights that obstetric surgical repairs, closures, and reconstructions are specialized uterine surgeries focused on addressing acute obstetric tissue injuries.

Obstetric repairs are organized as “OBSTETRICS: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS”, a subcategory of “repair of current obstetric laceration”. Exclusion rules prevent simultaneous coding of the therapeutic procedure “Irrigation of implanted venous access device for drug delivery systems” with a range of unrelated or overlapping interventions, including all gynecologic biopsies (“خزعة المبيض”, “خزعة بطانة الرحم”, etc.), other or therapeutic female‑organ procedures, repair of obstetric lacerations, “Excision of cervical stump by abdominal approach”, and “Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling”. Additional exclusions prohibit coding this irrigation procedure together with ultraviolet light therapy (المعالجة بالضوء فوق البنفسجي), platelet apheresis (فصادة الصفيحات), laparoscopic ectopic pregnancy treatment, or kidney transplant, thereby preserving coding clarity.
